coredata.dc:title: Microalgae-derived carbon quantum dots mediated formation of metal sulfide nano-adsorbents with exceptional cadmium removal performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chen
Bi, Lei
Liu, Jingzhang
Huang, Bang
Wang, Fengbang
Zhang, Yichang
Yao, Chongchao
Pan, Gang
Song, Maoyong
coredata.dc:description: Metal sulfides are regarded as efficient scavengers for heavy metals. However, the heavy metal adsorption capacity of metal sulfides is far from its theoretical values due to the insufficient exposure of adsorption sites. Surface modification of metal sulfides is considered one of the most effective strategies for improving heavy metal removal performance. Here, microalgae-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used as a green modifier for mediating nano-MnS/FeS formation to enhance Cd2+ removal. With the addition of 1 wt% CQDs, the Cd2+ adsorption capacity of 1 %CQDs-MnS reached 481 mg/g at 25 °C and 648.6 mg/g at 45 °C, which surpassed most of the previously reported metal sulfides. Furthermore, the CQDs-modified MnS displayed a better Cd2+ removal capacity than the commercial modifier sodium alginate. The mechanism analysis suggested that decreasing the particle size to expose more adsorption sites and providing additional chelating sites derived from the CQDs are two main reasons why CQDs enhance the Cd2+ adsorption capacity of metal sulfides. This study presents an exceptional cadmium nano-adsorbent of 1 %CQDs-MnS and provides a new perspective on the enhancement of heavy metal removal by using CQDs as a promising and universal green modifier that mediates the formation of metal sulfides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption of heavy metals
Surface modification
Carbon quantum dots
MnS
FeS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adsorption kinetics and isotherms of Cd2+
Effect of initial pH and temperature on adsorption performance
Comparative studies and release of manganese ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) The freeze-dried powder of CQDs; B) the photographs of CQDs dispersion under natural light and UV light of 365 nm; C) the FTIR spectra of CQDs.
Cd2+ adsorption capacity on modified MnS with various modifier/MnS mass ratios. Adsorbent dosage = 100 mg/L, initial concentration of Cd2+ =100 mg/L, pH = 6 and T = 25 °C.
A-C) SEM images of bare MnS, 1%SA, and 1% CQDs modified MnS, scale bars: 300 nm; D) hydrodynamic diameter of the MnS nanoparticles.

(A) adsorption kinetics of Cd2+; (B) adsorption kinetics parameter; (C) adsorption isotherm, adsorbent dosage = 100 mg/L, initial concentration of Cd2+ =10–600 mg/L, pH = 6 and T = 25 °C; (D) parameter in Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption model of Cd2+.
(A) and (B) Impact of temperature and initial pH on Cd2+ adsorption by 1% CQDs-MnS, respectively, adsorbent dosage = 100 mg/L, initial concentration of Cd2+ =100 mg/L.

(A) Comparison of maximum Cd2+ adsorption capacity for 1% CQDs-MnS with other adsorbents; (B) variation of cation concentration in the process of Cd2+ sorption by bare MnS and 1% CQDs modified MnS, adsorbent dosage = 1000 mg/L, initial concentration of Cd2+ =60 mg/L, pH = 6 and T = 25 °C.
FTIR spectra of the bare MnS, 1% CQDs-MnS, and 1% CQDs-MnS after Cd2+ adsorption.
XRD spectra of the 1% CQDs-MnS before and after Cd2+ adsorption.
XPS spectra for 1% CQDs-MnS before and after Cd2+ adsorption: (A) and (B) Mn 2p; (C) and (D) C 1s; (E) and (F) O 1s; (G) Cd 3d.
Possible mechanisms of Cd2+ removal by CQDs-MnS.

head.ce:title: Microalgae-derived carbon quantum dots mediated formation of metal sulfide nano-adsorbents with exceptional cadmium removal performance

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science, University of Copenhagen, Rolighedsvej 30, 1958 Frederiksberg, Denmark

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal sulfides are regarded as efficient scavengers for heavy metals. However, the heavy metal adsorption capacity of metal sulfides is far from its theoretical values due to the insufficient exposure of adsorption sites. Surface modification of metal sulfides is considered one of the most effective strategies for improving heavy metal removal performance. Here, microalgae-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used as a green modifier for mediating nano-MnS/FeS formation to enhance Cd2+ removal. With the addition of 1 wt% CQDs, the Cd2+ adsorption capacity of 1 %CQDs-MnS reached 481 mg/g at 25 °C and 648.6 mg/g at 45 °C, which surpassed most of the previously reported metal sulfides. Furthermore, the CQDs-modified MnS displayed a better Cd2+ removal capacity than the commercial modifier sodium alginate. The mechanism analysis suggested that decreasing the particle size to expose more adsorption sites and providing additional chelating sites derived from the CQDs are two main reasons why CQDs enhance the Cd2+ adsorption capacity of metal sulfides. This study presents an exceptional cadmium nano-adsorbent of 1 %CQDs-MnS and provides a new perspective on the enhancement of heavy metal removal by using CQDs as a promising and universal green modifier that mediates the formation of metal sulfides.

ce:section.ce:para: Cadmium (Cd) is a highly hazardous heavy metal that causes various diseases such as osteoporosis, nephropathy, and cancer [1]. Massive amounts of Cd have been discharged into the environment as a result of rapid industrial expansion. In China, 33.54% and 44.65% of arable and urban land have been polluted by Cd, making it the most polluted heavy metal in China [2]. Because of the similar chemical properties to Zn, Cd can be easily absorbed by animals and plants, which eventually leads to Cd2+ entering the human body through the food chain. It is well known that China needs to feed about 20% of the world’s population while only using 7% of the agricultural land [3]. To avert the great threat to food safety and human health, the development of effective interventions to eliminate Cd pollution is urgent.

Adsorption is believed to be one of the most effective and widely used techniques for the decontamination of heavy metals because it is inexpensive, highly efficient, and easy to operate [4,5]. Sulfur-containing adsorbents have attracted much attention because of their advantages of being common in nature, easy to synthesize, and having high treatment efficiency owing to the formation of insoluble metal sulfides [6,7]. Iron sulfide (FeS) is one of the earliest studied sulfur-containing adsorbents [8]. However, FeS shows poor performance in terms of the adsorption capacity of Cd2+. The search for other sulfur-containing adsorbents with high Cd2+ removal rates and the conversion of sulfur-containing adsorbents into nano-adsorbents with many more active sites are the two main strategies for the enhancement of Cd removal. In our previous study, we systematically investigated and compared the Cd2+ removal performance of different metal sulfides (FeS, MnS, MoS2, and ZnS), and found that MnS and ZnS presented a more superb Cd2+ adsorption capacity of 349 and 401 mg/g, respectively, than FeS (116 mg/g) [9–11]. With the development of nanotechnologies, nano-adsorbents have attracted the interest of researchers in environmental remediation due to their large specific surface area and numerous active sites [12]. However, because of their high surface energy, bare nanoparticles can easily agglomerate, leading to their deposition and deactivation [13]. Coating the surface of bare nanoparticles with a modifier (polymer or surfactant) is considered an effective strategy to limit their aggregation, as it provides spatial site resistance and electrostatic repulsion [14]. Carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) [15], starch [16], and sodium alginate (SA) [17] are common commercial modifiers used to control the growth and prevent the aggregation of FeS nanoparticles. In addition, unconventional and environmentally friendly bio-derivatives such as glucose, beef extract, gelatin, peptone, and yeast extract have been developed as stabilizers to modify FeS nanoparticles, some of which have great potential for application [18]. This is because these bio-derivatives have various charged functional groups that can provide electrostatic repulsion, and some of them are biomacromolecules that create steric hindrance effects by coating the surface of nanoparticles.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted widespread interest in recent years due to their superior properties, such as easy fabrication, colorful photoluminescence, excellent dispersibility, good chemical stability, superb biocompatibility, and non-toxicity [19]. Because of these unique properties, CQDs have been widely used in various fields, such as fluorescent sensors, bioimaging, photocatalysis, and electrocatalysis. Recently, there has been a trend to synthesize CQDs from various biomasses as they are easily available, inexpensive, renewable, green, and abundant compared to chemical reagents. The use of biomass not only fulfills the need for large-scale production of CQDs but also allows the conversion of low-value biomass into useful and valuable materials [20]. Biomass-derived CQDs have various functional groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, amine, and thiol groups, which are particularly suitable for the decoration and functionalization of nanomaterials. However, there are few reports on the enhancement of heavy metal removal by using CQDs as modifiers of nano-adsorbents.

In this study, CQDs with different functional groups were fabricated by hydrothermal treatment of renewable microalgal biomass, and the obtained microalgal-derived CQDs were used as a modifier to mediate the formation of MnS/FeS nano-adsorbents. Meanwhile, SA, a commercial modifier also derived from algae biomass, was used to modify MnS particles as a control group. We found that a small addition of CQDs had a significant prominent effect on Cd2+ adsorption by MnS. Moreover, the CQDs modified MnS displayed a better Cd2+ removal capacity than the commercial modifier SA, and outperformed most of the previously reported metal sulfides. The Cd2+ adsorption behavior (kinetics, isotherms, effect of pH and temperature, and ion release) on CQDs-MnS was systematically studied. The mechanisms for the enhanced Cd2+ removal induced by CQDs were investigated by various characterizations and discussed in detail.

Chlorella algae powder was purchased from Suzhou Baiyixin Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (China). Sodium alginate (SA), MnSO4, Na2S, and Cd(NO3)2 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co. Ltd. (China). All chemical reagents used were of analytical grade.

20 g of Chlorella algae powder was dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water. The mixed solution was then transferred to a reaction kettle and heated in a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 24 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the brown mixture was collected and centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 1 h to remove the impurity particles. Subsequently, the mixture was dialyzed in ultrapure water for two days with frequent water changes to remove impurities, such as ions and small molecule substances. Finally, the mixture was freeze-dried to obtain the CQDs powder, which was used for further studies.

MnS nanoparticles were prepared by co-precipitation under anaerobic conditions [21]. Briefly, a certain amount of modifier powder (SA or CQDs) was dispersed in 52.5 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 30 min. Then, the mixture was transferred to a three-neck flask and continuously aerated with 99 % N2 for 30 min to remove dissolved oxygen. Under continuous aeration with N2, 10 mL of MnSO4 solution (0.043 mol/L) was added to the three-neck flask, and 5 mL of Na2S solution (0.085 mol/L) was added dropwise with a partition funnel for 30 min under vigorous magnetic stirring. After the reaction, the solid product was collected by centrifugation (10000 rpm; 15 min), washed 3 times with deionized water, and then dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h. By adjusting the addition amount of modifiers, MnS nanoparticles with different modifier/MnS mass ratios of 0%, 0.25%, 0.67%, 1%, 2%, and 3% were prepared.

Based on the study of the impact of pH (Fig. 5B), the best Cd2+ adsorption performance on MnS was obtained at pH 6. The initial pH was adjusted to 6 in the following Cd2+ adsorption experiments. To determine the optimum dosage of modifiers and compare the improvement of Cd2+ adsorption by different modifiers, experiments on the Cd2+ removal capacity of MnS modified by SA and CQDs were conducted. The initial Cd2+ concentration was 100 mg/L, and the adsorbent dosage was 100 mg/L. The mixture was shaken at 120 rpm for 48 h at 25 °C.

To study the adsorption kinetics of adsorbents, the dosage of adsorbent was 100 mg/L, the initial Cd2+ concentration was 100 mg/L to simulate mining industry wastewater [22], the mixture was shaken at 120 rpm at 25 °C, and aliquots of the supernatant (5 mL) were collected at different contact times (0.08–48 h).

To study the adsorption isotherm, the adsorbent was used at a dosage of 100 mg/L with an initial Cd2+ concentration of 10–600 mg/L and continuous stirring at 25 °C for 48 h. After adsorption, 5 mL of supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the adsorbents.

To study the impact of pH and temperature on the adsorption capacity, the initial pH was adjusted from 4 to 7, and the adsorption temperature was tested from 15 to 45 °C. The dosage of adsorbent was 100 mg/L, and the initial Cd2+concentration was 100 mg/L, the mixture was shaken at 120 rpm for 48 h.

The concentrations of metal ions were determined by inductively coupled plasma-optical emission (ICP-OES) spectrometry (Perkin Elmer, Inc., USA). Each adsorption experiment was performed in triplicate.

The functional groups of the samples were analyzed using a Nicolet 8700 (Thermo-Fisher, USA) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. The morphology of metal sulfide nanoparticles was observed using an H-7500 scanning electron microscope (SEM; Hitachi, Japan). The particle size was investigated by using a Mastersizer 3000 analyzer (Malvern, United Kingdom) based on the dynamic light scattering (DLS) method. The chemical composition of the samples was determined by an X’Pert Pro MPD X-ray (Philips, The Netherlands) powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) diffractometer and ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Scientific, USA) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

The hydrothermal treatment of microalgal biomass at 200 °C produced a yellow supernatant solution. The supernatant was freeze-dried to obtain CQDs powder (Fig. 1 A). The CQDs powder can be easily mixed with water to obtain a clear solution that produces a blue emission color when illuminated by UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 1B), indicating that the CQDs have been successfully produced [23]. The FTIR spectra of the CQDs in Fig. 1C show the detection of bands at 3195, 3052, 2800–3000, 1656, 1446, 1400, 1292, 1044, 970, 766/651/616 cm−1 correlated to NH2, NH3, CHx, NH3 +, CH2/CH3, COO–, CN, C-OH, CS, and SH [24–26], respectively. FTIR data show that the CQDs possess a variety of functional groups including amino, methyl, carboxyl, cyano, hydroxyl, and thiol groups. We hypothesized that the diversity of functional groups could provide an abundance of active sites to enhance Cd2+ removal.

To determine the optional addition amount of modifiers, different amounts of modifiers were added during the fabrication process of MnS. Fig. 2 shows that the adsorption capacity of Cd2+ increased significantly with increasing CQDs content, reaching a maximum at about 1%, while the adsorption capacity decreased gradually at higher CQDs contents. Meanwhile, SA showed a similar trend but with a lower Cd2+ adsorption capacity. Therefore, 1% CQDs-MnS was chosen as the best adsorbent for the following study.

The morphology and dimensions of the modified MnS samples were investigated by SEM and DLS, respectively. In the absence of modifiers, the MnS particles showed significant aggregation with a diameter of about 150 nm (Fig. 3 A). In contrast, the size of the 1% SA/CQDs modified MnS particles decreased drastically as shown in Fig. 3B and C. Compared to SEM, DLS technology provided the hydrodynamic diameters of particles, that can reflect the actual dispersibility of the nanoparticles in solutions. Fig. 3D reveals that, among the three MnS nanoparticles, bare MnS had the largest hydrodynamic diameter of 141.8 nm, 1 %SA-MnS was smaller (37.8 nm), and 1% CQDs-MnS was the smallest (18.2 nm). Nanomaterials tend to form large agglomerates during synthesis to spontaneously minimize surface energy [27]. The SEM and DLS results demonstrated that CQDs can reduce the surface energy of MnS, resulting in the formation of smaller nanoparticles, and CQDs are a better stabilizer for nano-adsorbents than commercial SA. The smaller the particle size is, the more active sites are exposed. Therefore, we speculate that the CQDs modified MnS nanoparticles have the potential to show better Cd removal performance than bare MnS nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption kinetics and isotherms of Cd2+
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 A shows the comparison of the sorption kinetics of Cd2+ on bare MnS and 1% CQDs-modified MnS. Both treatments exhibited high initial Cd2+ uptake within the first 5 h. Thereafter, the adsorption rate gradually decreased and reached equilibrium within 48 h. This phenomenon is consistent with the general notion that the more accessible active sites are occupied first [28]. The pseudo-second-order kinetic model fits the kinetic data well, suggesting that chemisorption is a potential rate-limiting step for Cd2+ removal. The obtained fitted parameters are summarized in Fig. 4B.

The adsorption isotherms for MnS and 1% CQDs-MnS are shown in Fig. 4C. The adsorption data fit both the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models because of the high correlation coefficients (R2 > 0.97). However, the Freundlich model has a higher correlation coefficient than the Langmuir model, which implies that the adsorption of Cd2+ occurs on a heterogeneous surface [29]. The larger KL and KF values for 1% CQDs-MnS also indicated a better binding affinity for Cd2+ than for the bare MnS (Fig. 4D). The maximum adsorption capacity (qm) can be calculated from the Langmuir model. The qm of Cd2+ on 1% CQDs-MnS is nearly 1.7 times higher than that on bare MnS (Fig. 4D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial pH and temperature on adsorption performance
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of temperature on the adsorption capability of Cd2+ by 1% CQDs-MnS is presented in Fig. 5 A. It seemed that the higher temperature increased the Cd2+ adsorption capacity, and as the temperature increased from 15 to 45 °C, the Cd2+ adsorption capacity increased from 473.5 mg/g to 646.8 mg/g. This result implies that Cd2+ adsorption by 1% CQDs-MnS was thermodynamically favorable at temperatures of 15–45 °C.

The impact of the initial pH on the Cd2+ adsorption capacity by 1% CQDs-MnS is displayed in Fig. 5B. When the pH value was less than 3, we observed that the adsorbent partially dissolved. When the pH is above 8, Cd(OH)2 precipitates may occur spontaneously [37]; thus, adsorption experiments were conducted within a pH range of 4–7. The Cd2+ adsorption capability increased as the initial pH was elevated from 3 to 6 and reached a maximum at pH 6 but slightly decreased with further increasing pH to 7. Thus, according to the above results, the best Cd2+ adsorption performance on 1% CQDs-MnS was obtained at pH 6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative studies and release of manganese ions
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption capacity of Cd2+ on 1% CQDs-MnS was 481 mg/g at 25 °C and 648.6 mg/g at 45 °C, which surpassed most of the previously reported metal sulfides (293.8–401 mg/g) [9–11,30], MOFs (225.1 mg/g) [31], biochar (22.5 mg/g) [32], and microsphere (116.4 mg/g) [33] materials for Cd2+ decontamination (Fig. 6 A). Moreover, after adsorption of Cd2+ by bare MnS, the released amount of manganese ions reached several times the amount of removed Cd2+ (Fig. 6B). In contrast, the release of manganese ions was greatly inhibited after the modification of MnS by CQDs, while the amount of Cd2+ removed increased significantly (Fig. 6B). Cation exchange is considered to be the main mechanism for the removal of Cd2+ by metal sulfides [34]. However, the results of cation concentration changes in Fig. 6B show that in addition to cation exchange, chelation should also make a great contribution to the removal of Cd2+ by modifying bare MnS with CQDs, since the abundant functional groups on CQDs could provide abundant metal chelator binding sites. These metal chelator binding sites can also be used to chelate manganese ions. Therefore, the addition of CQDs significantly reduced the leaching risk of manganese ions from MnS and avoided secondary contamination.

FTIR spectroscopy technology was used to investigate the differences between the functional groups of bare MnS, 1% CQDs-MnS, and 1% CQDs-MnS after Cd2+ adsorption. Fig. 7 shows that in comparison with bare MnS, new peaks at 946, 1065, 1400, and 1649 cm−1 assigned to SH, C-OH, –COO-, and CO groups [35] appeared on 1% CQDs-MnS, respectively, thereby demonstrating that the CQDs had been successfully modified on the surface of MnS. The disappearance of SH and –COO- groups on 1% CQDs-MnS after adsorption of Cd2+ suggests that these reactive groups are involved in Cd2+ adsorption [26]. The involvement of CQDs with multiple functional groups not only provided steric hindrance to the aggregation of MnS [36] but also additional adsorption sites for the enhancement of Cd2+ removal on MnS.

The compositional variation of 1% CQDs-MnS before and after adsorption of Cd2+ was investigated by XRD, and the spectra are shown in Fig. 8 . Four diffraction peaks were observed in the pattern of 1% CQDs-MnS at 25.8°, 27.7°, 45.6°, and 54.8°, which were assigned to the (100), (002), (110), and (112) facets of MnS (JCPDS 40–1289), respectively. Otherwise, the peaks of manganese oxides (e.g. Mn2O3) and sulfur were present in the 1% CQDs-MnS, which are common impurities during the preparation of MnS [37]. After adsorption of Cd2+, the diffraction peaks of MnS and Mn2O3 disappeared. Meanwhile, new diffraction peaks belonging to CdS, CdMn2O4 and CdSO3 appeared, indicating that the formation of insoluble substances is one of the main mechanisms of the removal of Cd2+ by 1% CQDs-MnS.

The Mn 2p spectra shown in Fig. 9 A and B were best fitted with three curves, corresponding to Mn2+ (641.1), Mn3+ (642.5), and Mn4+ (644.9). The appearance of Mn3+ and Mn4+ indicated that partial oxidation of Mn2+ occurred on the surface of the 1% CQDs-MnS. The peak area ratios of Mn2+ decreased after adsorption, and the rate of Mn2+ decrease matched the rate of Mn3+ increase. Combined with the XRD results, CdMn2O4 was formed, and Mn2O3 disappeared after adsorption. Therefore, the pathways for the removal of Cd2+ on 1% CQDs-MnS could be described by the following reactions [37,38]:
MnS (s) + H+ = Mn2+ + HS-(1)
Cd2+ + HS- = CdS (s) + H+(2)
4Mn2+ + 2Cd2+ + O2 + 6H2O = 2CdMn2O4 (s) + 12H+(3)
Cd2+ + Mn2O3 (s) + H2O = CdMn2O4 (s) + 2H+(4)

The C 1 s spectrum in Fig. 9C and D was fitted with three components, including graphitic carbon/hydrocarbon (CC) at 284.7, phenol/alcohol/ether (CO) at 285.8, and carboxyl/ester at 288.8 [39]. Both peak area ratios for CO and CO decreased after adsorption, implying that the carbon functionalities from CQDs were involved in Cd2+ adsorption [39]. Moreover, the peak of O 1 s in Fig. 9E and F could be fitted with four peaks, corresponding to carbonyl/quinone/ketone/carboxyl (OC) at 532.2, hydroxyl/ether (OC) at 531.3, hydroxyl (OH) at 530.3 and M−O (M: Mn or Cd) at 528.8 [40]. The peak area ratios of OC and OH decreased after adsorption, indicating that these oxygen functionalities combined with Cd2+ during adsorption [41]. Meanwhile, the relative content of M−O increased from 16.8% to 22.3% after Cd2+ adsorption, which is further evidence of the formation of additional metal oxides (e.g. CdMn2O4) during the adsorption process. After adsorption, two characteristic peaks at binding energies of 405.4 eV and 412.1 eV were observed, which can be assigned to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 of Cd2+ in CdS [42].

Scheme 1 describes the possible mechanisms of Cd2+ removal by CQDs-MnS. During the formation of bare metal sulfides, cations could react with S2− to first form tiny metal sulfide nanocrystals with large surface energy. Then, these nanocrystals would self-aggregate into larger particles to minimize the surface energy [43]. After the addition of CQDs, a large amount of CQDs would cover the surface of MnS nanocrystals to reduce the surface energy and inhibit agglomeration significantly, resulting in the production of MnS nanoparticles with small sizes. These small nanoparticles can expose many more adsorption sites than large nanoparticles. This is one of the main reasons why CQDs enhance Cd2+ removal on MnS. Moreover, after the modification of CQDs, abundant functional groups from CQDs could be found on the surface of CQDs-MnS. These functional groups, such as carboxyl (–COOH), hydroxyl (–OH), and thiol (-SH), have been demonstrated to be involved in the combination with Cd2+ during adsorption. This is another reason for the enhancement of Cd2+ removal on MnS by CQDs. MnS particles could partially dissolve and release Mn2+ and HS- in a weakly acidic aqueous solution (reaction 1). HS- could react with Cd2+ to form more insoluble CdS (reaction 2). Meanwhile, Mn2+ can bind to Cd2+ to form CdMn2O4 (reaction 3). Mn2O3, which already exits or is newly generated from the oxidation of MnS, interacts with Cd2+ and forms CdMn2O4 (reaction 4). Therefore, the formation of precipitates (CdS and CdMn2O4) is the third reason for the removal of Cd2+ on 1 %CQDs-MnS. On the one hand, the formation of insoluble CdS and CdMn2O4 makes the CQDs modified MnS an excellent adsorbent with a high initial removal capacity of Cd2+. On the other hand, the recyclability of 1% CQDs-MnS is significantly limited, because of the formation of insoluble precipitates (Figure S1).

To demonstrate the universality of CQDs for the enhancement of Cd2+ removal on metal sulfides, we further modified FeS with CQDs and studied the effects on Cd2+ removal. It was found that the addition of CQDs could significantly inhibit the increase in FeS particle size (Figure S2 A and B), and the particle size could be reduced from 295.3 to 43.8 nm (Figure S2 C). Additionally, the maximum adsorption capacity of Cd2+ on 1% CQDs-FeS increased prominently, which was almost 2.2 times higher than that of bare FeS according to the calculations from the Langmuir model (Figure S3). In addition, Table S1 shows that the cost of 1 %CQDs-MnS is 3.1 $/kg, which is cheaper than most of the common adsorbents (e.g. carbon nanotubes, graphene, biochar, and commercial active carbon). Although the cost of CMC-FeS is lower than that of 1 %CQDs-MnS, the cost for removing 1 kg of Cd2+ (5.2 $/kg) is higher than that of the 1 %CQDs-MnS (4.8 $/kg). Therefore, the CQDs derived from microalgal biomass would be a promising and universal green modifier that could mediate the formation of cheap metal sulfide adsorbents for the enhancement of heavy metal removal.

In summary, we have demonstrated that CQDs derived from microalgal biomass as a green and efficient modifier of nano-adsorbents can enhance Cd2+ removal, as exemplified by modifying MnS particles with 1 wt% CQDs. 1 %CQDs-MnS showed a remarkably high Cd2+ adsorption capacity of 481 mg/g at 25 °C and 648.6 mg/g at 45 °C, which was not only 1.7 times higher than that of bare MnS (25 °C) but also surpassed most of the previously reported metal sulfides. Compared with commercial SA, CQDs modified MnS exhibits a better Cd2+ removal capacity. Mechanistic studies demonstrated that reducing the particle size of MnS to expose more adsorption sites and providing additional adsorption sites originating from the various functional groups of CQDs are two main reasons for the enhanced Cd2+ adsorption capacity of MnS by CQDs. Furthermore, CQDs can also be used to improve Cd2+ adsorption performance on other kinds of metal sulfides (e.g., FeS). These results reveal that CQDs modified MnS is an exceptional Cd2+ nano-adsorbent and that the microalgal-derived CQDs can be a promising and universal green modifier of metal sulfides to enhance the adsorption performance of heavy metals. Utilization of the microalgal-derived CQDs to modify other kinds of nano-adsorbents and assess their removal effect on a variety of heavy metals is worthy of further exploration.

Chen Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Methodology. Lei Bi: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Jingzhang Liu: Investigation, Methodology. Bang Huang: Investigation, Methodology. Fengbang Wang: Writing – review & editing. Yichang Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Chongchao Yao: Writing – review & editing. Gang Pan: Funding acquisition. Maoyong Song: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2021YFA0910300), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22125606, 21976198 and 21806175), the Strategic Priority Research Program of Chinese Academy of Sciences (XDPB2005) and the Special Project on Ecological Environmental Technology of Carbon Dioxide Emissions Peak and Carbon Neutrality (RCEES-TDZ-2021-21). Chen Wang and Yichang Zhang thank the China Scholarship Council (CSC) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.08.188.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
